shows_for_nwety.title: Friends
shows_for_nwety.description: Classic American sitcom with clear, conversational English. Perfect for picking up everyday phrases.

shows_for_nwety.title: Peppa Pig (English)
shows_for_nwety.description: Short episodes, simple vocabulary, very clearly pronounced. Don't feel embarrassed — loads of adult English learners use it.

shows_for_nwety.title: The Crown
shows_for_nwety.description: Beautiful British English. Slower-paced dialogue with rich vocabulary.

shows_for_nwety.title: Trevor Noah stand-up specials
shows_for_nwety.description: South African English with clear delivery — and he speaks some Portuguese too!

shows_for_nwety.title: Extra English
shows_for_nwety.description: A sitcom specifically designed for English learners.

shows_for_nwety_pt.title: Friends
shows_for_nwety_pt.description: Sitcom americana clássica, com inglês do dia-a-dia e diálogos claros. Óptima para apanhar frases úteis.

shows_for_nwety_pt.title: Peppa Pig (em inglês)
shows_for_nwety_pt.description: Episódios curtos, vocabulário simples e pronúncia muito clara. Não tenhas vergonha — muitos adultos aprendem inglês assim.

shows_for_nwety_pt.title: The Crown
shows_for_nwety_pt.description: Inglês britânico bonito, com diálogos mais calmos e vocabulário rico.

shows_for_nwety_pt.title: Especiais do Trevor Noah
shows_for_nwety_pt.description: Inglês sul-africano com entrega muito clara — e ele até fala um bocadinho de português!

shows_for_nwety_pt.title: Extra English
shows_for_nwety_pt.description: Uma sitcom feita especialmente para quem está a aprender inglês.

shows_for_you.title: 3% (Netflix original)
shows_for_you.description: Brazilian dystopian sci-fi — excellent Portuguese, though Brazilian accent.

shows_for_you.title: Glória
shows_for_you.description: Portuguese spy thriller set during the Cold War — European Portuguese, same variant as Mozambique.

shows_for_you.title: RTP Play (rtp.pt)
shows_for_you.description: Portugal's public broadcaster — tons of free European Portuguese content including news, which is the clearest pronunciation you'll find.

shows_for_you.title: Portuguese With Leo (YouTube)
shows_for_you.description: A Portuguese guy teaching European Portuguese with subtitles — start here.

shows_for_you.title: Easy Portuguese (YouTube)
shows_for_you.description: Street interviews with subtitles in Portuguese and English. Real, casual speech.

tips_english: English stress usually falls on the first syllable of a word. Say TA-ble, not ta-BLE.
The 'th' sound doesn't exist in Portuguese — put your tongue between your teeth. Practice with 'think' and 'this'.
English has no grammatical gender. 'The' is just 'the' — no o/a, um/uma. This is one less thing to worry about.
We use 'do' / 'does' to form questions: 'Do you like music?' — Portuguese doesn't do this, so it feels weird at first.
Contractions (I'm, you're, don't) sound more natural than the full forms in conversation.
When in doubt, add '-ing' for ongoing actions: 'I am learning' instead of 'I learn'.
tips_english_pt: Em inglês, a tónica cai quase sempre na primeira sílaba. Diz TA-ble, não ta-BLE.
O som 'th' não existe em português — põe a ponta da língua entre os dentes. Pratica com 'think' e 'this'.
O inglês não tem género gramatical. 'The' é sempre 'the' — não há o/a, um/uma. Menos uma coisa para te preocupares!
Usamos 'do' / 'does' para fazer perguntas: 'Do you like music?' — em português não funciona assim, por isso estranha-se no início.
As contrações (I'm, you're, don't) soam mais naturais do que as formas completas na conversa.
Na dúvida, acrescenta '-ing' para acções em curso: 'I am learning' em vez de 'I learn'.
tips_portuguese: European Portuguese swallows vowels — 'pequeno' sounds more like 'pkenu'. Don't worry, this is normal.
Nouns have gender: 'o livro' (the book, masculine), 'a casa' (the house, feminine). Learn the article with the noun.
'Tu' is informal 'you' (used with family and friends — use it with Nwety). 'Você' is more formal.
Verbs change based on who's doing the action: eu amo, tu amas, ele ama. Memorise verbs in groups, not alone.
'Saudade' has no exact English translation — it's a mix of longing, missing someone, and nostalgia all at once. It's a beautifully Portuguese concept.
Mozambican Portuguese uses some unique words: 'machimbombo' for bus, 'bazar' for to leave quickly. Standard European Portuguese is understood everywhere though.
